subtitle: Journal-club style ICU update: PICO, key results, appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English.

script.heading: Intro
sentences: Welcome to Critical Care Daily for July 02, 2026.
Today we will review 3 recent ICU-relevant publications.
The format is quick listen first, then PICO, key results, critical appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English notes.
Use this as a morning triage tool, then open the source link for full-text appraisal before changing practice.

script.heading: Story 1 - Noninvasive Respiratory Support for Adult Patients with Acute
sentences: Story 1 is about Noninvasive Respiratory Support for Adult Patients with Acute Respiratory Failure. An Official American Thoracic Society Clinical Practice Guideline..
This is a recent research article from American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in Critically ill or ICU-relevant patients described in the abstract; confirm eligibility criteria in the full text., how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?

The panel made a strong recommendation for HFNC and a conditional recommendation for NIV or CPAP for adults with acute hypoxemic respiratory failure with close monitoring for the need for escalation of respiratory support, based primarily on effects on need for intubation.
The panel made a strong recommendation for HFNC or NIV for preoxygenation prior to endotracheal intubation to prevent peri-intubation hypoxemia.

For acute hypercapnic respiratory failure, the panel made a strong recommendation for NIV to reduce mortality and need for invasive mechanical ventilation, and a conditional recommendation for HFNC only in patients with less severe hypercapnia and with mild acidemia (eg, pH > 7.25), provided that close monitoring and prompt escalation to NIV are available.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 2 - Early high-dose vitamin C for out-of-hospital cardiac arrest:
sentences: Story 2 is about Early high-dose vitamin C for out-of-hospital cardiac arrest: the VITaCCA randomized clinical trial..
This is a randomized / clinical trial from Intensive care medicine.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in adults resuscitated from shockable out-of-hospital cardiac arrest randomly received intravenously placebo, supplementary (3 g) or supraphysiological dose (10 g) vitamin C daily for 96 h, how should we interpret the 10 g group (p = 0.04 for overall difference). 10 g vitamin C resulted in 2.5 points less improvement in R-SOFA score compared with placebo (95% CI 0.5-4.5; p = 0.01), and 1.6 points less improvement compared with 3 g vitamin C (95% CI - 0.4 to 3.6; p?
273 patients (93 on placebo, 91 on 3 g and 89 on 10 g) were included in the primary analysis.

10 g vitamin C resulted in 2.5 points less improvement in R-SOFA score compared with placebo (95% CI 0.5-4.5; p = 0.01), and 1.6 points less improvement compared with 3 g vitamin C (95% CI - 0.4 to 3.6; p = 0.12).
Mean (SD) change in R-SOFA score at 96 h was - 3.2 (5.7) in the placebo group, - 2.3 (7.4) in the 3 g group, and - 0.8 (7.6) in the 10 g group (p = 0.04 for overall difference).
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use this for journal club before practice change; decide whether the measured outcome is important enough for bedside decisions.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 3 - Association of White Blood Cell Count with Treatment Response
sentences: Story 3 is about Association of White Blood Cell Count with Treatment Response to Cefepime vs Piperacillin-Tazobactam..
This is a recent research article from American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in Critically ill or ICU-relevant patients described in the abstract; confirm eligibility criteria in the full text., how should we interpret Association of White Blood Cell Count with Treatment Response to Cefepime compared with Piperacillin-Tazobactam?

There was a significant interaction between WBC and treatment group in predicting 28-day mortality in both the ACORN trial and the IV study (odds ratio [OR] 0.95, 95% confidence interval [CI] 0.92-0.98, p = <0.01 and OR 0,95, CI 0.94-0.96, P < .01, respectively), with likelihood ratio testing revealing better model fit with inclusion of the interaction term (F = 8.17, p = <0.01 and F = 42.9, P < .01, respectively).

In the ACORN cohort, patients with a baseline WBC count of 16 or higher had significantly lower odds of mortality when treated with piperacillin-tazobactam compared to cefepime (OR 0.51, CI 0.29-0.90).
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: This may refine antibiotic selection in selected infected patients, but it should be integrated with local microbiology, renal risk, stewardship, and source control.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Outro
sentences: That wraps up today's journal club style update.
Before applying any finding, check the full source, the absolute effect size, and your local ICU context.
For English practice, repeat one academic phrase from each article out loud.

articles.title: Noninvasive Respiratory Support for Adult Patients with Acute Respiratory Failure. An Official American Thoracic Society Clinical Practice Guideline.
articles.source: American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine
articles.summary: The panel made a strong recommendation for HFNC and a conditional recommendation for NIV or CPAP for adults with acute hypoxemic respiratory failure with close monitoring for the need for escalation of respiratory support, based primarily on effects on need for intubation. The panel made a strong recommendation for HFNC or NIV for preoxygenation prior to endotracheal intubation to prevent peri-intubation hypoxemia. Acute hypoxemic and hypercapnic respiratory failure are among the most common reasons for ICU admission and need for invasive mechanical ventilation. Noninvasive respiratory support (NIRS) strategies-including high-flow nasal cannula (HFNC), noninvasive ventilation (NIV), and continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP)-may prevent intubation, improve outcomes, and reduce ICU utilization. However, there is uncertainty regarding optimal patient and modality selection, resulting in variable implementation.
articles.analysis: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.

pico.population: Critically ill or ICU-relevant patients described in the abstract; confirm eligibility criteria in the full text.
pico.intervention: Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective.
pico.comparator: Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.
pico.outcomes: The panel made a strong recommendation for HFNC and a conditional recommendation for NIV or CPAP for adults with acute hypoxemic respiratory failure with close monitoring for the need for escalation of respiratory support, based primarily on effects on need for intubation. For acute hypercapnic respiratory failure, the panel made a strong recommendation...

keyResults: The panel made a strong recommendation for HFNC and a conditional recommendation for NIV or CPAP for adults with acute hypoxemic respiratory failure with close monitoring for the need for escalation of respiratory support, based primarily on effects on need for intubation.
The panel made a strong recommendation for HFNC or NIV for preoxygenation prior to endotracheal intubation to prevent peri-intubation hypoxemia.

For acute hypercapnic respiratory failure, the panel made a strong recommendation for NIV to reduce mortality and need for invasive mechanical ventilation, and a conditional recommendation for HFNC only in patients with less severe hypercapnia and with mild acidemia (eg, pH > 7.25), provided that close monitoring and prompt escalation to NIV are available.

The panel also issued a risk-based recommendation, suggesting HFNC for low-risk patients and NIV for high-risk patients to reduce the need for re-intubation following extubation after critical illness.
criticalAppraisal: Early evidence: useful for awareness, not enough alone to change practice.
articles.practiceImpact: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.

articles.title: Early high-dose vitamin C for out-of-hospital cardiac arrest: the VITaCCA randomized clinical trial.
articles.summary: This trial investigated whether early administration of 3 g or 10 g intravenous vitamin C attenuated organ dysfunction post-cardiac arrest. In this double-blind, multi-center, phase 2 trial, comatose adults resuscitated from shockable out-of-hospital cardiac arrest randomly received intravenously placebo, supplementary (3 g) or supraphysiological dose (10 g) vitamin C daily for 96 h. 273 patients (93 on placebo, 91 on 3 g and 89 on 10 g) were included in the primary analysis. 10 g vitamin C resulted in 2.5 points less improvement in R-SOFA score compared with placebo (95% CI 0.5-4.5; p = 0.01), and 1.6 points less improvement compared with 3 g vitamin C (95% CI - 0.4 to 3.6; p = 0.12). Besides temperature management, there is currently no effective therapy to decrease the burden of post-cardiac arrest syndrome.
articles.analysis: Use this for journal club before practice change; decide whether the measured outcome is important enough for bedside decisions.

pico.population: adults resuscitated from shockable out-of-hospital cardiac arrest randomly received intravenously placebo, supplementary (3 g) or supraphysiological dose (10 g) vitamin C daily for 96 h
pico.intervention: the 10 g group (p = 0.04 for overall difference). 10 g vitamin C resulted in 2.5 points less improvement in R-SOFA score
pico.comparator: placebo (95% CI 0.5-4.5; p = 0.01), and 1.6 points less improvement compared with 3 g vitamin C (95% CI - 0.4 to 3.6; p
pico.outcomes: 273 patients (93 on placebo, 91 on 3 g and 89 on 10 g) were included in the primary analysis. Mean (SD) change in R-SOFA score at 96 h was - 3.2 (5.7) in the placebo group, - 2.3 (7.4) in the 3 g group, and - 0.8 (7.6) in the 10 g group (p = 0.04 for overall difference). 10 g vitamin C resulted in 2.5 points less improvement in R-SOFA score compared with...

keyResults: 273 patients (93 on placebo, 91 on 3 g and 89 on 10 g) were included in the primary analysis.

10 g vitamin C resulted in 2.5 points less improvement in R-SOFA score compared with placebo (95% CI 0.5-4.5; p = 0.01), and 1.6 points less improvement compared with 3 g vitamin C (95% CI - 0.4 to 3.6; p = 0.12).
Mean (SD) change in R-SOFA score at 96 h was - 3.2 (5.7) in the placebo group, - 2.3 (7.4) in the 3 g group, and - 0.8 (7.6) in the 10 g group (p = 0.04 for overall difference).
Vitamin C 10 g led to higher troponin T release, worse renal function and worse neurological outcomes.
criticalAppraisal: Interventional evidence: check allocation, blinding, protocol adherence, and absolute effect size.
Check randomization, concealment, blinding, missing data, and whether analysis was intention-to-treat.
articles.practiceImpact: Use this for journal club before practice change; decide whether the measured outcome is important enough for bedside decisions.

articles.title: Association of White Blood Cell Count with Treatment Response to Cefepime vs Piperacillin-Tazobactam.
articles.source: American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine
articles.summary: Secondary outcomes in the secondary analysis of the ACORN trial included highest stage of acute kidney injury on an ordinal scale, a composite outcome of major adverse kidney events, and incidence of coma or delirium within 14 days. There was a significant interaction between WBC and treatment group in predicting 28-day mortality in both the ACORN trial and the IV study (odds ratio [OR] 0.95, 95% confidence interval [CI] 0.92-0.98, p = <0.01 and OR 0,95, CI 0.94-0.96, P < .01, respectively), with likelihood ratio testing revealing better model fit with inclusion of the interaction term (F = 8.17, p = <0.01 and F = 42.9, P < .01, respectively). In the ACORN cohort, patients with a baseline WBC count of 16 or higher had significantly lower odds of mortality when treated with piperacillin-tazobactam compared to cefepime (OR 0.51, CI 0.29-0.90). Empiric anti-pseudomonal coverage is an important part of treatment of sepsis, but there is limited data to guide antibiotic selection. The antibiotic choice on renal outcomes (ACORN) randomized trial found no difference in mortality when comparing cefepime and piperacillin-tazobactam, while an Instrumental Variable (IV) study found improved mortality with cefepime.
articles.analysis: This may refine antibiotic selection in selected infected patients, but it should be integrated with local microbiology, renal risk, stewardship, and source control.

In the ACORN cohort, patients with a baseline WBC count of 16 or higher had significantly lower odds of mortality when treated with piperacillin-tazobactam compared to cefepime (OR 0.51, CI 0.29-0.90).
